provenance.upstream_generation_method: curated starter baseline with declared framings
provenance.review_method: Model-assisted structural review of the four supplied evidence notes and declared framing policies; no systematic full-text verification of the cited corpus

evidence.title: Moderate egg consumption and population-level cardiovascular risk
evidence.claim: At a population level, moderate egg consumption—roughly up to one egg per day—has not shown a consistent association with higher cardiovascular-disease risk across prospective-cohort meta-analyses, although results vary by population and study design.
evidence.evidence_quality: The BMJ paper includes large prospective cohorts and a systematic review, but the evidence is observational and vulnerable to residual confounding, dietary-measurement error, and variation in what foods eggs replace or accompany. The AHA advisory is an expert synthesis rather than an independent experimental result.
evidence.strongest_counterargument: Other large observational analyses have reported positive associations between dietary cholesterol or egg intake and cardiovascular outcomes. Aggregating population averages may also conceal meaningful individual response differences and subgroup effects.
evidence.confidence_assessment: No numeric confidence is assigned in this starter baseline. The warranted conclusion is conditional: moderate intake is not consistently associated with higher population-level cardiovascular risk, but this does not establish individual safety or settle questions about other health outcomes.

citations.citation: Drouin-Chartier, J.-P., et al. (2020). Egg consumption and risk of cardiovascular disease: three large prospective US cohort studies, systematic review, and updated meta-analysis. *BMJ* 368:m513. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.m513

Carson, J. A. S., et al. (2020). Dietary Cholesterol and Cardiovascular Risk: A Science Advisory From the American Heart Association. *Circulation* 141:e39–e53. https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000743

Zhong, V. W., et al. (2019). Associations of Dietary Cholesterol or Egg Consumption With Incident Cardiovascular Disease and Mortality. *JAMA* 321(11):1081–1095. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2019.1572

framing_assumptions: ‘Good to eat’ is operationalized as population-level cardiovascular risk at typical consumption levels.
policy_sensitivities: The answer changes with outcome definition, geography, dietary comparator, exposure measurement, and the treatment of observational confounding.
coverage_gaps: Individual response, non-cardiovascular health outcomes, animal welfare, environmental impact, and systematic non-English coverage are outside this evidence note.
correlation_risks: The advisory and meta-analytic evidence synthesize overlapping bodies of observational research and should not be treated as wholly independent confirmation.
capability_limits: Assessing sampling, ascertainment, and spatial or temporal inference requires raw data and specialist statistical review beyond the supplied summary.

evidential: Prospective cohorts remain vulnerable to residual confounding and dietary-measurement error.
ingestion_conditional: The starter corpus selects a small English-language cardiovascular evidence set rather than enumerating the full health literature.
capability_conditional: A complete dietary-epidemiology appraisal would require study-level bias and substitution analysis.
framing_conditional: A population-average cardiovascular result cannot answer the broader unqualified question by itself.

notes: This is the interpretation a silent baseline is most likely to adopt, which is precisely why the framing must be declared.
This is a substantive structural annotation of the supplied evidence note, not an independent finding that its factual claim is true.
